national_day_of_spain.intro: Spain's national day on 12 October marks the 1492 arrival of Columbus in the Americas and celebrates Spanish identity and its global ties.
national_day_of_spain.history: The date recalls 12 October 1492, when Columbus first reached the Americas under the Crown of Castile, beginning Spain’s global expansion. It coincides with the feast of the Virgin of the Pilar, patroness of Spain, and became the official national holiday in 1987.
national_day_of_spain.traditions: A large military parade passes through Madrid before the King and royal family, with a flyover trailing the red and yellow of the flag. In Zaragoza the day blends with the famous Fiestas del Pilar.
funFacts: 12 October is celebrated across the Hispanic world as Día de la Hispanidad.
The red-and-yellow smoke trail over Madrid is a parade highlight.
It shares the date with the feast of the Virgen del Pilar in Zaragoza.

constitution_day.intro: 6 December celebrates the 1978 constitution that established Spain's democracy after the Franco dictatorship.
constitution_day.history: On 6 December 1978 Spaniards approved the new constitution by referendum, completing the transition to democracy after Franco’s death in 1975. It created a parliamentary monarchy, regional autonomy and fundamental rights.
constitution_day.traditions: The Congress of Deputies in Madrid opens to the public, and institutions hold ceremonies and educational events. With the Immaculate Conception on 8 December it forms the ‚puente de diciembre‘, a popular long weekend.
funFacts: The 1978 constitution passed with about 88% approval.
The December ‚puente‘ is one of Spain’s busiest travel weekends.
